CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Question Does the fact that B is disqualified as a result of A’s protest make B’s protest against C invalid? Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). cases.titulo: Virada completada libre por proa en la zona
cases.relato_protesta: Y vira en la zona de la marca de barlovento por delante de B. Y completa su virada y queda libre por proa, pero B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: Y vira en la zona. B debe caer para evitarla después de que Y completó su virada .
Norma: Regla 12 e interpretación TR CALL E6 .
Rationale: Si Y completa su virada sin obligar a B a maniobrar durante la misma (Regla 13) y sin riesgo de contacto inmediato (Regla 15), B ahora es el barco que viene por popa y debe mantenerse alejado .
Decisión: Sin penalización para Y.
cases.query: Y vira en la zona de la marca de barlovento por delante de B. Y completa su virada y queda libre por proa, pero B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la Situación

En el incidente descrito, Y gira en la zona de la marca de barlovento antes que B y completa su virada quedando libre por proa. Sin embargo, B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y. Este escenario implica una serie de reglas de vela que deben ser consideradas: las reglas 12, 14(a), y 18.2(a)(2) son particularmente relevantes.

## Sección 2: Citas Regulatorias

- **Regla 12**: Esta regla establece la obligación de una embarcación a mantenerse claramente alejada de otra embarcación que tiene derecho de vía. En este caso, B debe mantenerse claramente alejada de Y.
  
- **Regla 14(a)**: Establece que una embarcación debe evitar cualquier contacto con otra embarcación si es posible hacerlo sin comprometer su posición en la regata.

- **Regla 18.2(a)(2)**: Esta regla requiere a la embarcación que está libre por proa (en este caso, Y) de dar espacio para el paso al barco que está libre por popa (B).

## Sección 3: Análisis y Razonamiento

En el momento en que Y gira en la zona de la marca de barlovento, B aún no ha llegado a la zona. Según Regla 18.2(a)(2), Y debe dar espacio para el paso a B. Sin embargo, cuando Y completa su virada y queda libre por proa, B se encuentra en una situación donde debe mantenerse claramente alejada de Y (Regla 12). Si B no puede evitar chocar con la popa de Y, entonces B no está cumpliendo con su obligación de mantenerse claramente alejada.

Además, según Regla 14(a), si es posible para una embarcación evitar el contacto sin comprometer su posición en la regata, debe hacerlo. En este caso, si B puede caer inmediatamente y evitar el choque con Y, entonces B está cumpliendo con esta regla.

## Sección 4: Decisión

Dado que B no pudo mantenerse claramente alejada de Y (Regla 12) y no evitó el contacto cuando era posible hacerlo sin comprometer su posición en la regata (Regla 14(a)), se debe penalizar a B.

**Decisión: Penalizar a B.**

Regla 18.2(a)(2) obliga a la embarcación libre por proa a dar espacio para el paso al barco que está libre por popa.
Cuando Y completa su virada, queda libre por proa y B debe mantenerse claramente alejada de Y (Regla 12).

In that situation the boat clear ahead is required by rule 18.2(a)(2) to give mark-room to the boat clear astern. Facts A and B were both on port tack, reaching to a mark to be left to starboard. The wind was light. At position 1, when B reached the zone, A was clear ahead of B but four-and-a-half hull lengths from the mark. Between positions 1 and 2 A gybed and headed to the mark, becoming overlapped outside B. Then B gybed and headed to the mark. Between positions 2 and 3, after B had gybed and turned towards the mark, she became clear ahead of A. When B first became clear ahead of A there was about one-half of a hull length of open water between the boats. A few seconds after B became clear ahead, A, who was moving faster, struck B on the transom. There was no damage or injury. B protested A under rule 12 and 18.2(a)(2). A was disqualified and she appealed. Decision At position 1, B was the first of the two to reach the zone, even though she was clear astern of A. Therefore, rule 18.2(a)(2) applied, and it required A, which had not reached the zone at that moment, to give mark-room to B, which was room for B to sail to and past the mark on the required side in a seamanlike way. There are no facts that suggest B sailed outside of the mark-room to which she was entitled after position 1, or that she broke rule 10 while on port tack or rule 15 after gybing and becoming clear ahead of A. If she were to have broken rule 10 or rule 15, she would be exonerated by rule 43.1(b) for those breaches. When A hit B’s transom, she obviously was not keeping clear of B, and so it was proper to disqualify A for breaking rule 12. A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. The appeal is dismissed, and the protest committee’s decision is upheld. Finally, A broke rules 12, 14(a) and 18.2(a)(2) and she remains disqualified. USA 1962/87; revised by World Sailing 2025

cases.titulo: Contacto antes de completar la virada tras cruce
cases.relato_protesta: B (estribor) cruza por delante de Y (babor). Después de cruzar, B orza y vira a babor. Durante la virada, B choca con Y antes de llegar a rumbo de ceñida.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: B cruza a Y. B vira y choca con Y antes de completar la virada .
Norma: Regla 13 según TR CALL D4 .
Rationale: B perdió su derecho de paso al virar. Mientras un barco vira, debe mantenerse alejado de los demás .
Decisión: Penalizar a B.
cases.query: B (estribor) cruza por delante de Y (babor). Después de cruzar, B orza y vira a babor. Durante la virada, B choca con Y antes de llegar a rumbo de ceñida.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la situación

En el incidente descrito, B cruza por delante de Y y luego orza hacia babor durante su virada. Durante esta maniobra, B choca con Y antes de completar su rumbo de ceñida. Es necesario determinar si B incumplió alguna regla al realizar esta acción.

## Sección 2: Citas de las Reglas y Casos Relevantes

1. **Regla 16.1 (TR CALL A3)**:
   - Esta regla establece que una embarcación que debe mantenerse clara debe hacerlo de manera inmediata y en forma marinera al cambiar el rumbo del barco con derecho de vía.
   
2. **TR CALL A3**:
   - En este caso, se describe la situación donde B y Y están superpuestas y B responde a los cambios de curso de Y de manera inmediata y marinera. Si B no puede mantenerse clara debido a las acciones de Y, entonces es Y quien incumple la regla 16.1.

3. **TR CALL A2**:
   - Esta interpretación indica que una embarcación debe mantener un curso con suficiente distancia para evitar cualquier contacto inmediato y cambiar el rumbo en ambas direcciones sin causar contacto.

## Sección 3: Análisis de la Aplicabilidad

- Según **TR CALL A2**, B estaba superpuesta con Y antes de realizar su virada. Al orzar, B no mantuvo suficiente distancia para evitar un contacto inmediato con Y.
  
- De acuerdo a **Regla 16.1** y **TR CALL A3**, si una embarcación (en este caso Y) cambia el curso y la otra embarcación (B) no puede mantenerse clara, entonces es responsabilidad de B hacer todo lo posible para establecer suficiente distancia entre ambas embarcaciones.

- En este incidente, B orzó antes de completar su rumbo de ceñida, lo que indica una maniobra inadecuada y sin consideración a la posición de Y. Esto sugiere que B no actuó de manera marinera al realizar esta acción.

## Sección 4: Decisión

Dado el análisis anterior, se concluye que B incumplió las reglas al orzar antes de completar su rumbo de ceñida y causar un contacto con Y. Por lo tanto:

**Decisión: Penalizar a B.**

TR CALL A1 — 13 While Tacking; 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. As neither boat established this overlap from clear astern, the overlap is said to be 'instantaneous'. Rule 17 does not apply and B may sail above her proper course. A tacking boat changes tack the moment she passes head to wind. If another boat becomes overlapped to leeward of her from clear astern during the tacking boat passes head to wind, rule 17 applies and the leeward boat may not sail above her proper course.

TR CALL A2 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? Answer B is keeping clear of Y when Y, the right-of-way boat, can sail her course with no need to take avoiding action and also, because they are overlapped, change course in both directions without immediately making contact. At positions 1 & 2: B is keeping clear. At position 3: if Y luffs, there will be contact but not immediately, so B is still keeping clear. At position 4: Y cannot change course to windward without immediately making contact with B. B is no longer keeping clear, and would be penalized under rule 11 if Y were to protest, whether or not the boats make contact. At any moment, a boat’s course (as that term is used in Part 2 and the Definitions) means the direction in which her bow is pointing. It does not mean her proper course, nor the course she could be expected to be sailing shortly. There does not need to be contact for a boat to fail to keep clear.

retrieved.text:
TR CALL A3 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped; 16.1 Changing Course

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Rule 16.1 Changing Course Definitions Keep Clear Question Y and B are overlapped, sailing parallel courses, with about ½ boat’s width between them. Y has no proper course restriction. Just before position 3 Y luffs s lowly and B promptly responds. Y continues to luff and there is no longer any means of B avoiding Y. B tries to keep clear by luffing and there is contact. Y protests. What should the call be?

Answer Positions 1 and 2: B is keeping clear because she is su fficiently far from Y that Y is able to change course in both directions without immediately making contact. Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Question Does the fact that B is disqualified as a result of A’s protest make B’s protest against C invalid? Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). GBR 1962/25

cases.relato_protesta: Tres barcos (B, X, Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. X es el barco más a sotavento y líder. Y y B están solapados por fuera. X frena bruscamente para "atrapar" a B y permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior. B choca con X.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: X frena en la marca para atrapar a B. B choca con X .
Norma: Regla 11 y 18.2 según TR CALL J9 .
Rationale: X es el barco con derecho de paso y espacio de marca. B (barlovento) debe mantenerse alejada de X independientemente de la táctica de equipo de X .
Decisión: Penalizar a X.
cases.query: Tres barcos (B, X, Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. X es el barco más a sotavento y líder. Y y B están solapados por fuera. X frena bruscamente para "atrapar" a B y permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior. B choca con X.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la Situación

En este incidente, tres barcos (B, X, y Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. El barco X es el más a sotavento y líder en la carrera. Los barcos Y y B están solapados por fuera. En un momento determinado, X frena bruscamente para permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior de B, lo cual resulta en una colisión entre B y X.

## Sección 2: Citas de las Reglas

1. **Regla 14**: Evitar Contacto
   - Esta regla establece que un barco debe evitar cualquier contacto con otro barco siempre que sea posible.
   
2. **TR CALL A3 (Regla 11, Regla 16.1)**: Mantenerse a Distancia
   - Según esta llamada de caso, si dos barcos están solapados y uno cambia de curso, el barco que debe mantenerse a distancia debe responder prontamente y de manera marinera para evitar cualquier contacto.

3. **Regla 18.2(a)(2)**: Dar Espacio al Mar
   - Esta regla requiere que un barco en posición claramente a popa dé espacio suficiente a otro barco para pasar el mar sin obstáculos.

## Sección 3: Análisis de la Aplicación de las Reglas

1. **Regla 14**: En este caso, X frena bruscamente y provoca una colisión con B. Esto indica que X no cumplió con su obligación de evitar cualquier contacto posible con otro barco.

2. **TR CALL A3 (Regla 11, Regla 16.1)**: Al momento en que Y luce para pasar por el interior y B intenta mantenerse a distancia, la responsabilidad recae sobre X como el barco más a sotavento de frenar adecuadamente para evitar cualquier contacto.

3. **Regla 18.2(a)(2)**: Dado que X es claramente a popa de B en este contexto y frena bruscamente, no cumple con la obligación de dar espacio suficiente a B para pasar el mar sin obstáculos.

## Sección 4: Decisión Final

Dadas las circunstancias descritas y la aplicación de las reglas mencionadas, se concluye que X es responsable del incidente por no cumplir con sus obligaciones bajo Regla 14 y Regla 18.2(a)(2).

**Decisión: Penalizar a X.**

claims.claim: X frena bruscamente para permitir que Y pase por el interior de B.
Esta acción resulta en una colisión entre B y X.
Regla 14 establece que un barco debe evitar cualquier contacto con otro barco siempre que sea posible.
Según TR CALL A3, si dos barcos están solapados y uno cambia de curso, el barco que debe mantenerse a distancia debe responder prontamente para evitar cualquier contacto.
Regla 18.2(a)(2) requiere que un barco en posición claramente a popa dé espacio suficiente a otro barco para pasar sin obstáculos.
X no cumplió con su obligación de evitar el contacto según Regla 14.
Al momento de la colisión, X era responsable de mantenerse a distancia según TR CALL A3.
X no cumplió con la obligación de dar espacio suficiente a B para pasar el mar sin obstáculos según Regla 18.2(a)(2).
La responsabilidad del incidente recae sobre X por no cumplir con sus obligaciones bajo Regla 14 y Regla 18.2(a)(2).
Se concluye que X es responsable del incidente.
X debe ser penalizado por el incidente.

A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. cases.query: Y navega por la amura de babor. B navega por la amura de estribor. Y tiene que virar para evitar a B, pero durante la maniobra el tripulante de B empuja la botavara hacia afuera deliberadamente para tocar a Y.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la Situación

Y navega por la amura de babor y B por la amura de estribor. Durante la maniobra de viraje que realiza Y para evitar el contacto con B, un tripulante de B empuja deliberadamente la botavara hacia afuera, provocando una colisión.

## Sección 2: Citas Legales y Reglas Aplicables

1. **Regla 14 (Evitar Contacto):** Esta regla establece que cada competidor debe evitar cualquier contacto con otro barco en la medida de lo posible.
   
2. **TR CALL A3:** Este llamado se refiere a situaciones donde dos barcos están superpuestos y uno tiene la obligación de mantenerse claro del otro. Si el barco que debe mantenerse claro responde prontamente y de manera marinera, no incumple la regla.

3. **Regla 16.1 (Cambiar de Rumbo):** Esta regla protege a un barco que está obligado a mantenerse claro si responde prontamente y de manera marinera a cualquier cambio de rumbo del barco con derecho de paso.

4. **Regla 43.1(c) (Exoneración):** Si el contacto no causa daño ni lesiones, la regla exonera al barco que es el derecho de paso en caso de incumplimiento.

## Sección 3: Análisis Jurídico

- **Responsabilidad de Y:** Aunque Y tuvo que virar para evitar a B, su maniobra fue legítima y no violó ninguna regla. Sin embargo, la acción del tripulante de B empujando deliberadamente la botavara hacia afuera provocó el contacto.

- **Responsabilidad de B:** La acción del tripulante de B es claramente una violación de la regla 14, ya que su intención era causar un contacto deliberado. Además, al empujar la botavara, B no estaba actuando de manera marinera y provocó el contacto.

- **Exoneración:** Dado que Y no tuvo opción para evitar el contacto una vez que B empujó la botavara hacia afuera, Y está exonerada por regla 43.1(c) ya que no causó daño ni lesiones y era el barco con derecho de paso.

## Sección 4: Decisión

Dadas las circunstancias descritas en los relatos y la aplicación de las reglas mencionadas, se concluye lo siguiente:

- **B** violó la regla 14 al provocar deliberadamente el contacto.
- **Y** no incumplió ninguna regla ya que su maniobra fue legítima y respondió prontamente a la acción de B.

Por tanto, la decisión es clara:

`Decisión: Penalizar a B.`

TR CALL A1 — 13 While Tacking; 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. As neither boat established this overlap from clear astern, the overlap is said to be 'instantaneous'. Rule 17 does not apply and B may sail above her proper course. A tacking boat changes tack the moment she passes head to wind. If another boat becomes overlapped to leeward of her from clear astern during the tacking boat passes head to wind, rule 17 applies and the leeward boat may not sail above her proper course.

TR CALL A2 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? Answer B is keeping clear of Y when Y, the right-of-way boat, can sail her course with no need to take avoiding action and also, because they are overlapped, change course in both directions without immediately making contact. At positions 1 & 2: B is keeping clear. At position 3: if Y luffs, there will be contact but not immediately, so B is still keeping clear. At position 4: Y cannot change course to windward without immediately making contact with B. B is no longer keeping clear, and would be penalized under rule 11 if Y were to protest, whether or not the boats make contact. At any moment, a boat’s course (as that term is used in Part 2 and the Definitions) means the direction in which her bow is pointing. It does not mean her proper course, nor the course she could be expected to be sailing shortly. There does not need to be contact for a boat to fail to keep clear.

TR CALL A3 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped; 16.1 Changing Course

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Rule 16.1 Changing Course Definitions Keep Clear Question Y and B are overlapped, sailing parallel courses, with about ½ boat’s width between them. Y has no proper course restriction. Just before position 3 Y luffs s lowly and B promptly responds. Y continues to luff and there is no longer any means of B avoiding Y. B tries to keep clear by luffing and there is contact. Y protests. What should the call be?

Answer Positions 1 and 2: B is keeping clear because she is su fficiently far from Y that Y is able to change course in both directions without immediately making contact. Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

cases.query: B y Y están en el tramo de popa, solapados en la misma amura. B es sotavento y tiene restricción de rumbo debido (Regla 17). B traslucha a la amura de babor y vuelve a trasluchar a estribor de inmediato para orzar a Y.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la Situación

B y Y están en el tramo de popa, solapados en la misma amura. B es sotavento y tiene restricción de rumbo debido a que está en una situación donde aplica la Regla 17. En este contexto, B realiza varias trasluchas rápidas: primero cambia a babor y luego inmediatamente vuelve a estribor para orzar hacia Y.

## Sección 2: Citación de las Reglas Aplicables

- **Regla 17**: Esta regla establece que una embarcación sotavento en un tramo de popa tiene restricciones en su capacidad para cambiar de rumbo.
  
- **TR CALL A3 (Regla 16.1)**: Este llamado explica la responsabilidad de una embarcación que debe mantenerse clara y cómo debe responder a los cambios de curso del barco con derecho de vía.

- **TR CALL A2 (Reglas 11, 14, 15, 18.2(a)(2))**: Este llamado proporciona un ejemplo similar en el que una embarcación sotavento debe mantenerse clara y dar espacio al barco con derecho de vía.

## Sección 3: Análisis Jurídico

Según la Regla 17, B tiene restricciones en su capacidad para cambiar de rumbo debido a su posición como sotavento. Sin embargo, B realiza varias trasluchas rápidas y cambia de amura sin considerar adecuadamente el impacto sobre Y.

- **TR CALL A3** indica que una embarcación con restricciones de curso (como B) debe responder de manera oportuna y marinera a los cambios del barco con derecho de vía. En este caso, B no actuó de forma seamanlike al realizar trasluchas rápidas sin considerar la posición de Y.

- **TR CALL A2** sugiere que una embarcación sotavento debe mantenerse clara y evitar cualquier contacto inmediato con el barco con derecho de vía. B no cumplió esta obligación ya que sus movimientos provocaron un riesgo de contacto con Y.

## Sección 4: Decisión Final

Dado que B realizó varias trasluchas rápidas sin considerar adecuadamente la posición y los derechos del barco sotavento, se concluye que B no actuó de manera seamanlike y violó las reglas aplicables.

**Decisión:** Penalizar a B.

Therefore, rule 18.2(a)(2) applied, and it required A, which had not reached the zone at that moment, to give mark-room to B, which was room for B to sail to and past the mark on the required side in a seamanlike way. There are no facts that suggest B sailed outside of the mark-room to which she was entitled after position 1, or that she broke rule 10 while on port tack or rule 15 after gybing and becoming clear ahead of A. If she were to have broken rule 10 or rule 15, she would be exonerated by rule 43.1(b) for those breaches. When A hit B’s transom, she obviously was not keeping clear of B, and so it was proper to disqualify A for breaking rule 12. A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. The appeal is dismissed, and the protest committee’s decision is upheld. Finally, A broke rules 12, 14(a) and 18.2(a)(2) and she remains disqualified. USA 1962/87; revised by World Sailing 2025

Norma: Regla D1.1(e) según TR CALL K1 .

cases.query: Y ha terminado la regata y está navegando de regreso. En su camino, pasa cerca de B que aún está compitiendo, obligando a B a virar para evitarla.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Análisis de la Situación

Y ha terminado la regata y está navegando de regreso cuando pasa cerca de B, que aún está compitiendo. Como resultado, B es obligada a virar para evitar una colisión con Y.

## Sección 2: Citas Reglamentarias Aplicables

1. **Regla 14**: Evitar el Contacto
   - Esta regla establece que una embarcación debe hacer todo lo posible para evitar un contacto peligroso y, si no puede ser evitado, debe minimizar cualquier daño o perjuicio.

2. **TR CALL A3**:
   - Este llamado se refiere a la situación en la cual dos barcos están superpuestos y uno de ellos (Y) cambia de rumbo para mantener distancia con el otro (B). Si B responde prontamente y de manera marinera, Y no puede continuar ladeando sin infringir regla 16.1.

3. **Regla 18.2(a)(2)**:
   - Esta regla establece que una embarcación que llega a la zona del obstáculo después de otra debe recibir espacio para pasar el obstáculo en la dirección requerida de manera marinera. En este caso, aunque no se trata directamente de un obstáculo, refuerza el concepto de dar espacio y evitar contactos innecesarios.

## Sección 3: Análisis del Incidente

En el momento del incidente:
- Y está regresando después de terminar la regata.
- B aún está compitiendo activamente en la regata.
- La proximidad entre las dos embarcaciones obliga a B a virar para evitar un contacto con Y.

Dado que Y ya ha finalizado su participación en la regata, no tiene el mismo compromiso de seguir las reglas como si aún estuviera compitiendo. Sin embargo, es importante considerar que Y debe actuar de manera marinera y evitar causar problemas innecesarios a otras embarcaciones que aún están compitiendo.

## Sección 4: Decisión Final

Dado el análisis del incidente y las reglas aplicables:
- **Decisión**: Penalizar a Y.
   - A pesar de que Y ya ha terminado la regata, su acción de pasar cerca de B obligando a esta última a virar para evitar un contacto infringe regla 14. Además, según TR CALL A3, si Y continúa ladeando sin dar espacio suficiente a B, está infringiendo reglas adicionales.

**Decisión: Penalizar a Y.**

TR CALL A1 — 13 While Tacking; 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. As neither boat established this overlap from clear astern, the overlap is said to be 'instantaneous'. Rule 17 does not apply and B may sail above her proper course. A tacking boat changes tack the moment she passes head to wind. If another boat becomes overlapped to leeward of her from clear astern during the tacking boat passes head to wind, rule 17 applies and the leeward boat may not sail above her proper course.

retrieved.text:
TR CALL A2 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? Answer B is keeping clear of Y when Y, the right-of-way boat, can sail her course with no need to take avoiding action and also, because they are overlapped, change course in both directions without immediately making contact. At positions 1 & 2: B is keeping clear. At position 3: if Y luffs, there will be contact but not immediately, so B is still keeping clear. At position 4: Y cannot change course to windward without immediately making contact with B. B is no longer keeping clear, and would be penalized under rule 11 if Y were to protest, whether or not the boats make contact. At any moment, a boat’s course (as that term is used in Part 2 and the Definitions) means the direction in which her bow is pointing. It does not mean her proper course, nor the course she could be expected to be sailing shortly. There does not need to be contact for a boat to fail to keep clear.

retrieved.text:
TR CALL A3 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped; 16.1 Changing Course

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Rule 16.1 Changing Course Definitions Keep Clear Question Y and B are overlapped, sailing parallel courses, with about ½ boat’s width between them. Y has no proper course restriction. Just before position 3 Y luffs s lowly and B promptly responds. Y continues to luff and there is no longer any means of B avoiding Y. B tries to keep clear by luffing and there is contact. Y protests. What should the call be?

Answer Positions 1 and 2: B is keeping clear because she is su fficiently far from Y that Y is able to change course in both directions without immediately making contact. Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Question Does the fact that B is disqualified as a result of A’s protest make B’s protest against C invalid? Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). GBR 1962/25

cases.query: B (estribor) y Y (babor) en ceñida. B está claramente por delante y Y pasaría por su popa sin problemas. B decide virar justo en la proa de Y, obligando a Y a virar de inmediato para evitar el choque.
cases.answer:
## Sección 1: Resumen de la Situación

B y Y navegaban en ceñida, con B claramente por delante de Y. En un momento dado, B decidió virar justo frente a la proa de Y, lo que obligó a Y a virar inmediatamente para evitar el choque.

## Sección 2: Citas Legales y Reglas Aplicables

1. **Regla 14**: Evitar Contacto
   - Esta regla establece que una embarcación de derechas debe evitar cualquier contacto con otra embarcación de izquierdas si es posible hacerlo sin sacrificar su propia posición en la carrera.

2. **TR CALL M10 (D2.3)**: Decisiones Iniciadas por los Árbitros
   - Esta sección establece que los árbitros pueden penalizar a una embarcación que no cumple correctamente con las reglas de Parte 2, incluyendo la obligación de dar claridad y repetir señales visuales y vocales.

3. **TR CALL M10 (D1.1(d))**: Cambios en las Reglas de Parte 2
   - Esta sección establece que los árbitros deben actuar según si no hubo una señal clara y repetida, incluso si la embarcación Y intentó dar claridad.

4. **Case 27 (Regla 15)**: Adquirir Derecho de Paso
   - Este caso establece que cuando una embarcación adquiere derecho de paso debido a su propia acción, la otra embarcación es obligada a darle espacio para mantenerse clara.

## Sección 3: Análisis y Argumentos

1. **Análisis de la Situación**:
   - B decidió virar justo frente a Y, lo que forzó a Y a tomar medidas inmediatas para evitar el choque.
   
2. **Aplicación de Regla 14**:
   - Según la regla 14, una embarcación en posición de derechas debe esforzarse por evitar cualquier contacto con otra embarcación de izquierdas si puede hacerlo sin sacrificar su propia posición en la carrera.
   
3. **Aplicación del TR CALL M10 (D2.3)**:
   - Los árbitros pueden penalizar a una embarcación que no cumple correctamente las reglas, incluyendo la obligación de dar claridad y repetir señales visuales y vocales.
   
4. **Aplicación del Case 27**:
   - Cuando B viró para adquirir derecho de paso, Y estaba en su derecho de esperar que B le diera espacio suficiente para mantenerse clara.

## Sección 4: Decisión Final

Dada la situación descrita y las reglas aplicables, se concluye que:

- **B** forzó a **Y** a tomar medidas inmediatas para evitar un choque.
- Según la Regla 14 y el TR CALL M10 (D2.3), B no cumplió con su obligación de dar claridad y espacio suficiente a Y.

Por lo tanto, se decide que:

**Decisión: Penalizar a B.**

Esta decisión refleja que B incumplió la regla 14 al forzar a Y a tomar medidas inmediatas para evitar un choque sin darle el tiempo y el espacio necesario para mantenerse clara.

TR CALL C3 — 21.1 Starting Errors; 23.2 Interfering with Another Boat

Rule 21.1 Starting Errors Rule 23.2 Interfering with Another Boat Question After the start, B is sailing on port tack towards the pre -start side of the starting line without having started. Y has started correctly and bears away below her proper course onto a collision course with B. B gybes and keeps clear of Y. B protests. What should the call be? Answer Penalize Y. At position 1, B is keeping clear of Y as required by rule 21.1. When Y bears away after position 1 she breaks rule 23.2 as she is no longer sailing her proper course and she interferes with B, a boat subject to rule 21.1. After the starting signal, if a boat is sailing towards the pre -start side of the line, the umpires will use the principle of the ‘last point of certainty’ and decide the boat has not started, unless they are certain that she has started.

TR CALL L5 — 21.2 Taking a Penalty; 44.2 One-Turn and Two-Turns Penalties

Rule 21.2 Taking a Penalty Rule 44.2 One-Turn and Two-Turns Penalties Rule D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions Question 1 How will the umpires decide that a boat has taken a penalty correctly under rule 44.2? Answer 1 A boat takes a penalty correctly when, as soon as possible after an incident or after being penalized by the umpires, she first sails well clear and then makes the required number of turns. The answers below clarify these requirements. Question 2

A and B are opponents of Y. Rule 17 does not apply to Y who has been penalized. A has to alter course and slow down to avoid Y and protests. What should the call be?

Answer 2 Although Y breaks no rule of Part 2, she fails to sail clear as soon as possible. In position 1 she is able to slow down, fall astern of both B and A and then sail clear. Penalize Y under rule D2.3(e). Question 3 When is a boat subject to rule 21.2? Answer 3 Rule 21.2 begins to apply when a boat is clearly turning in order to take a penalty. It ceases to apply when the boat completes the last required tack or gybe or when she stops taking her penalty before it is complete.

SECTION L – OTHER RULES Question 4 When may the umpires take action against a boat that has failed to take a penalty correctly? Answer 4 The umpires should penalize a boat that fails to take a penalty correctly as follows (a) under rule D2.3(e) when the boat has clearly indicated that she will take a penalty and breaks rule D1.3(b); (b) under rule D2.3(e) when the penalty was signalled by an umpire and the boat breaks rule D2.5. In both cases, the failure may be a failure to get well clear as soon as possible, or a failure to take the penalty correctly in accordance with rule 44.2. A right-of-way boat that is sailing to get clear retains right of way until rule 21.2 starts to apply to her. A boat that interferes with another boat while sailing clear, when she has a clear alternative, does not sail clear as soon as possible.

TR CALL M10 — D1.1(d) Changes to the Rules of Part 2; D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions

Rule D1.1(d) Changes to the Rules of Part 2 Rule D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions Question 1 The sailing instructions state that rule D1.1(d) applies requiring clear and repeated arm signals, in addition to hails. If there is an arm signal but no hail from Y, what action should the umpires take a) if B protests? b) on their own initiative? Answer 1 a) When the umpires are certain that no hail for room to tack was made, then the signal for room to tack has not been made. (The umpires may be certain there was no hail if they have had no difficulty hearing other calls from a similar position.) For the purpose of the rules of Part 2 they should act as if there has been no signal. b) The umpires may decide that Y has broken rule D2.3(f) and penalize her. They could also, in an extreme or repeated case, display a black and white flag to report the incident to the protest committee. Question 2 If there is a hail but no arm signal, is the answer the same? Answer 2 Yes.

CASE 20 — When it is possible that a boat is in danger, another boat that

Rule 1.1, Safety: Helping Those in Danger Rule 61.4(b)(4), Redress: Redress Decisions When it is possible that a boat is in danger, another boat that gives help is entitled to redress, even if her help was not asked for or if it is later found that there was no danger. Facts Dinghy A capsized during a race and seeing this dinghy B sailed over to her and offered help. A accepted help and B came alongside, taking the crew of two aboard. Then all hands worked for several minutes to right A, whose mast was stuck in the mud. Upon reaching shore, B requested redress under rule 61.4(b) (4). The protest committee considered several factors in its decision. First, A’s helmsman was a highly experienced sailor. Secondly, the wind was light, and the tide was rising and would shortly have lifted the mast free. Thirdly, she did not ask for help; it was offered. Therefore, since neither boat nor crew was in danger, redress was refused. B appealed, stating that rule 1.1 does not place any onus on a boat giving help to decide, or to defend, a decision that danger was involved. Decision B’s appeal is upheld. A boat in a position to help another that may be in danger is required by rule 1.1 to do so. It is not relevant that a protest committee later decides that there was, in fact, no danger or that help was not requested. B is entitled to redress. The protest committee is directed to reopen the hearing and to grant appropriate redress following the requirements and advice given in rules 61.4(c), 61.4(d) and A9. GBR 1968/14

CASE 27 — A boat is not required to anticipate that another boat will break

Rule 2, Fair Sailing Rule 13, While Tacking Rule 14, Avoiding Contact Rule 15, Acquiring Right of Way Rule 43, Exoneration A boat is not required to anticipate that another boat will break a rule. When a boat acquires right of way as a result of her own actions, the other boat is entitled to room to keep clear. Facts When AS reached the zone, she was clear ahead of BP. Between position 1 and 2, AS, a hull length to leeward and a hull length ahead of BP, tacked as soon as she reached the starboard-tack lay line. Almost immediately she was hit and damaged by BP travelling at about ten knots. The protest committee disqualified AS for breaking rule 15. It also disqualified BP under rule 2, pointing out that she knew AS was going to tack but did nothing to avoid a collision. BP appealed, asserting that she was not obligated to anticipate an illegal tack. Decision After AS reached the zone, BP was required by rule 12 to keep clear of her and by rule 18.2(a)(2) to give her mark-room. Both these obligations ended when AS passed head to wind (see rules 18.1(a)(1) and 18.2(b)). When AS passed head to wind, BP became the right-of-way boat under rule 13 and held right of way until AS assumed a close-hauled course on starboard tack. At that moment AS, having just acquired right of way under rule 10, was required by rule 15 to give BP room to keep clear. The collision occurred almost immediately after AS assumed a close-hauled course on starboard tack. Therefore, BP needed to take avoiding action before AS had borne away to a close-hauled course. At that time BP had right of way under rule 13, and so AS broke rule 13. It is a principle of the right-of-way rules, as stated in rule 15, that a boat that becomes obligated to keep clear by an action of another boat is entitled to sufficient time and space to respond. When AS acquired right of way under rule 10, she did not give BP room to keep clear and broke rule 15. Finally, AS broke rule 14(a) because she could have avoided the contact by turning back onto port tack after she passed head to wind. BP took no action to avoid the collision, but what could she have done? The second sentence of rule 14 states that a right-of-way boat need not act to avoid contact until it is clear that the other boat is not keeping clear . Given her speed and the distance involved after it became clear that AS was not keeping clear, BP had perhaps one to two seconds to decide what to do and then do it. While it was obvious that AS would eventually tack to round the mark, no rule required BP to anticipate that AS would break a rule. BP did break rule 10, but she was exonerated for that breach by either rule 43.1(a) or rule 43.1(b). BP did not break rule 14 because it was not reasonably possible for her to have avoided the collision after AS broke rule 13. BP did not violate any principle of sportsmanship or fair play and, therefore, did not break rule 2. BP’s appeal is upheld. She is to be reinstated. AS remains disqualified. USA 1971/140
